comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: That you know off. Do the beings we call gods communicate to other entities outside of the solar system?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have no idea. I don't think any human being knows.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Many, many thanks for Magic Monday,

I've been doing regular mediation and ritual now since February and it feels like more and and more gunk has been expelled from my subtle bodies in a 'detox'. It's exhausting! But feels very important to my development as a person. It really is wonderful even if it is simultaneously hard and extremely painful at times.

As this process continues, I've been feeling by a sense that there is something I need to *do*, that feels important for my Individuality -- but I have next to no idea what that is! Again, I imagine that this experience of a spiritual 'waiting room' is normal and healthy (although of course I'm not sure), and I so I ask you do you know of a good way of figuring out what that important thing that needs doing is? Or if it's just a matter of doing *something*? Or is it best to wait until there is clarity? Meditating on some different ideas has led me to think many of the ideas are worthwhile, but I've not yet had the emotional satisfaction of finding the 'right' one, the one that clicks and makes my life make more sense. This too, though, could be an unduly limiting illusion, and so I'm asking for your experienced opinion.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It isn't about "doing." The best metaphor I've yet encountered for the work of spiritual development is the way you grow a plant from a seed. You don't have to "do" the growing; you provide the seed with the conditions it needs -- soil, water, a suitable temperature -- and it does the growing all by itself, without strain or stress. In the same way, you provide your spirit with the right conditions through appropriate practices, and it unfolds in its own time.

In the same way, and even more importantly, there is no "right" idea. The process of meditation will lead you to explore many ideas, and one of the things you'll learn is the very imperfect fit between human ideas and reality. The process is the thing that matters.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Violetcabra's question resonates deeply with me, and your answer is very helpful. Thank you!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What is the general internal outcome of western esoteric practices? You said "Eastern" goals of "enlightenment" or "awakening" aren't the only game in town last week. I know each tradition probably leads to a different mountain but if Buddhism is aimed towards "awakening" an individual through, for instance, direct experience of "no-self" what is the aim of, for instance, Golden Dawn training?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In the Golden Dawn tradition, the self we normally experience, the ordinary personality or ego, is called the Lower Self. There are two higher aspects of the self, one of which is called the Higher Self, and the other the Higher and Divine Genius -- the word "genius" used to mean a guardian spirit before it meant somebody with unusual mental talents. The Higher Self consists of all the potentialities of the individual that we don't yet experience, while the Higher and Divine Genius is our link with the divine. The goal of Golden Dawn training, then, is to unite the Lower and Higher Selves, so that the initiate is able to access the whole range of his or her potentialities and is no longer stuck in the limited views of the ego. When this happens, the combined Self is in direct contact with the Higher and Divine Genius, receiving its guidance and energies.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear jmg
Have people ever used magic to discipline or control their children? (E.g. make them stop having tantrums, want to succeed in the career the parents chose for them ect)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No doubt, but I don't recommend it. Dominating anyone's will using magic is a slippery slope that leads to very unwelcome places.

replies.title: magic on kids
replies.content:
Years ago I was told in passing that the Book of Shadows of a tradition related to the one I was studying (part of Central Valley Wicca) had a whole collection of spells for different purposes. One was supposedly "to keep a child quiet." Would definitely be a best seller if it worked. I never actually saw the book in question, so who knows what it involved. But the idea got a lot of laughs from the moms in the group. We were mostly "Yeah right, if only." :)



Rita

comments.title: Meme Wars
comments.content: What do you think of fashionable liberal women invoking Kauket to “counterbalance” Kek and restore the old order that supported their worldview?

replies.title: Re: Meme Wars
replies.content:
I think they need to learn a lot more about Egyptian mythology. "Counterbalance" does not mean "oppose;" Kek and Kauket are a syzygy, a pair of balanced energies sharing a common function, and if they invoke Kauket and strengthen her influence, that'll simply make it easier for that common function to manifest itself in the world.

It's quite possible that the Kek-Kauket syzygy is luring them into this work...

replies.title: Re: Meme Wars
replies.content: So… magic works.  But not how you think it should…  Is something different / similar going on with the shrill insistence that (select some bit of modernity – like a fashionably bohemian lifestyle) WILL TOO survive peak fossil fuels?  (Alternatively – the plenty o’oil line.)  Frequently, the speakers seem stuck in the denial and anger stages of Kübler-Ross’s stages of grief, and direct nasty rage at anyone offering disagreement, including ways out.  What is going on here?

replies.title: Re: Meme Wars
replies.content: I think it's much the same. A lot of people these days are caught in a brutal cognitive dissonance between the future of endless linear progress to which they think they're entitled, and a universe that's serenely uninterested in giving that to them. Donald Trump, the return of rising oil prices, and all the other signs of social and ecological blowback are impossible for them to deal with, because none of that's supposed to happen -- after all, they're the good people, doing the right things, and so the world should just keep on giving them all the comforts they expect, including the comfort of believing that things will just keep on slowly getting better forever...and since that's not happening, and it's becoming increasingly clear that it's not going to happen, yeah, shrieking meltdowns are the order of the day.

comments.title: Egregor?
comments.content: I’m the cheerful fool writing a fraternal rite for the Green Wizards’ Benevolent and Protective Association.  The project is getting its own dark and harvest-y personality.  (Possibly because we are gleaning ideas in the autumn of modernity…?)  So, should we arrange meetings around harvest aspects of the Moon and astrological signs?  Rusty

replies.title: Re: Egregor?
replies.content: In a perfect world, sure. In the world we live in, it usually works best to choose a date on the ordinary calendar -- for example, the third Thursday of every month, or what have you.

comments.title: 3 questions
comments.content:
1) what do you think of Aurum Solis? Are they credible? And would you recommend certain individuals avoid them for a specific reason (like mental illness, for example)?

2) In the spirit of the other blog, what is the earliest use of magic by European settlers in the Americas that you know of?

3) (this one is for the community at large) in a previos magic monday, a poster conplained about their tarot deck which was air-wands and fire-swords. What deck was that? Or can anyone recommend tarot decks with those associations?

Thanks!!!
Sincerely
Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content:
1) To the best of my knowledge it's a solid and fully functional magical system, very close to that of the Golden Dawn. As with any other system of ceremonial magic, it should be avoided by people who have mental problems sufficiently severe that they need more than the mildest kind of medication, or any kind of serious heart problem.

2) All the European settlers who crossed the Atlantic brought their own folk magic with them; I don't recall what exactly the oldest magical working was, but the English settlements, for example, had plenty of ordinary English folk magic going as soon as they arrived.

3) I'll leave that for the readership!

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content:
Aeclectic is a large database of tarot decks and reviews. You can sort for decks with reversed attributions thus:

http://www.aeclectic.net/tarot/cards/changed-suit-element-associations.shtml

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content: I understand mental problems, but why do you recommend people with heart issues avoid ceremonial magic?

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content: Handling magical energies can put serious strain on the physical and etheric bodies; when I finish a really intense ritual, I feel roughly as though I'd just done a workout with heavy barbells. If your heart can't handle that kind of strain, ceremonial magic is a bad idea.

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content:
You've been very straightforward about the first warning on your blogs (first time I see the second), but I think that if such a thing exists on TCGD, it's not in one of the expected places. Suggestion for a new edition?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content: Johannes Kelpius and his followers, thought by some to have been Rosecrucians, arrived in Philadelphia in 1694 and set up the Hermits or Mystics of the Wissahickon.  They offered regular programs of study and meditation, as well as public worship and medical treatment.

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content:
I'm the poster with the reversed attribution tarot deck -I wasn't complaining, I like my deck!

It's The Witches Tarot by Ellen Cannon-Reed. She makes other changes on cards attrubuted to different paths, and the meaning of The Priestess, for example, is through her Wiccan framework; though I'm not Wiccan I find it works tolerably well. She also cross references the Rider-Waite and Golden Dawn symbology, for those looking into her rationale for changes. The most significant being of course that she doesn't follow Waite having exclusively negative interpretations of swords in any presentation (and of course, the fire switch). She takes an approach where while they may be harsh, they're medicine in a positive presentation (the negative attribution is in the negative presentation).

My only beef, really, is her husband's super 80s artwork for people's hairdos! ;-)

replies.title: Re: 3 questions
replies.content:
3. I'm pretty sure that The modern spellcasters tarot uses these associations.
Sacred Seed and The Truth in Story both have YouTube run throughs of the deck and accompanying book

comments.title: Atlantis redux
comments.content:
JMG,

Continuing with the Atlantis theme: I've come across speculation that at some point in their civilizational development the Atlanteans acquired a great racial pride, probably stemming from their tech/psychic prowess which gave them easy sway over other nations and peoples. Flash forward some 16 thousand years or so to late 19th-early 20th century when, it is speculated, a great number of former Atlanteans were incarnated in .... Germany. Great racial pride? Check. Tech prowess? Check. Fascination with the occult and the psychic realm? Check. Desire to hold sway over Europe, if not the entire world? Definitely.

I know that in German occult circles, "Thule" was considered the Germanic mythic Homeland, but could they have been conflating this with Atlantis? Do you suppose such a mass incarnation of former Atlanteans could have occurred in Germany, even though there may have been no bloodline ties with Atlantis? Or did the rise and fall of Nazi Germany basically manifest a destructive Atlantean archetype, a fate to which all peoples might be subject?

Thanks in advance - Will M

replies.title: Re: Atlantis redux
replies.content: Hmm. Where did you encounter this? Dion Fortune insisted that a lot of Atlanteans were incarnating in late 19th and early 20th century Britain, and I think it was Edgar Cayce who believed that lots of them were incarnating in early 20th century America. Given how long ago Atlantis was, I'd doubt that anyone who was around then is still around now...

replies.title: Re: Atlantis redux
replies.content:
Monsters is one of my favorite books because of its lusus serius approach. I like how some concepts are introduced there.

In the same way, I will speculate on this reincarnation, based on how Buddhism seeks to reincarnate people, with techniques developed to enhance memory retention between incarnations.

I will guess that aspects of one's prior lives are brought back by the context of the current life. A person would remember aspects of a past life as a hunter in the current one, even if there as no connection with it for several lives.

I guess that maybe the Atlanteans had some sort of method like this, or not; in any case, the current cultural environment is very similar to the one that brought the downfall of Atlantis due to climate change and exploitation of technology; it would be reasonable to expect that this similarity would resonate with the Atlantean mindset and it would tend to "bring them back". The examples mentioned in the post and in your reply seem to point that the resurgence of Atlanteans is global.

replies.title: Re: Atlantis redux
replies.content: Possibly, but again, if the same souls are still here after 11,600 years, something's gone very wrong. My guess is that those souls have long since gone on, and the resonances being experienced now are of a subtler kind.

replies.title: Re: Atlantis redux
replies.content:
Well, people are claiming to be Atlanteans. It may be because the subject got revived by theosophists; it also might be the Good People and their hijinks yet again--they have been quite active in the UFO scene, why not Atlantis too? Whatever people believe, it is what they will get from glamour.

That, of course, does not discard your interesting idea that something might have gone deeply, horribly wrong.

replies.title: Re: Atlantis redux
replies.content:
I'd have to guess - it's something I came across in my early 20's - maybe 30 years ago - when I was gobbling down anything on metaphysics and the occult I could lay my mitts on - but it's probably the Bergier-Pauwels co-authored Morning Of The Magicians.

It's refreshing to think that it doesn't take more than 13 thou years for a soul, no matter how many missteps or karmic barnacles accumulated, to graduate to the upper realms! I wonder if there's an average time that a soul remains earth-bound.

Speaking of earth-bound souls (post-mortem), I was wondering if you or any of your readers have read the novel "Lincoln In The Bardo" by George Saunders. Hard to describe briefly, but it concerns earth-bound spirits in a cemetery where Lincoln's deceased young son Willie Todd is suddenly introduced to the crowd , and how that not only helps them to gain spiritual release but offers them (and the reader) insights into the mind of Abe and his early prosecution of the Civil War. The novel is a very compassionate slice of Americana, sort of a metaphysical "Our Town" - and the author clearly did his research into post-mortem states of mind. Also it's very well written, not pulpy at all (not that there's anything wrong with pulp!). Quite remarkable read, in my opinion.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What happens with magic and karmic backlash if the working you do won't kick in until after your death? Like the Ancient Egyptian 'if anyone messes with my tomb, may he be eaten by crocodiles' kind of thing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The act of will and the movement of energies still take place in the present, and so the Raspberry Jam Principle applies at once, irrespective of when the spell is meant to take effect.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: With your understanding of reincarnation, how does the Buddhist goal of getting off the wheel of life and death work? Is it another way of describing reaching Gwynfydd or is it something else entirely?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd probably have to sit down with a Buddhist and work out the comparison from first principles. According to Druid lore, the dwellers in Gwynfydd no longer lose the thread of consciousness and memory when their bodies change, and of course their bodies are of a different kind than those of dwellers in Abred, but there's no end point, no point where things just stop -- no upper limit to the power and wisdom and grace you can earn by your own efforts.

comments.title: Druidry and Hermeticism
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

Your comment on converting Hermetic spiritual techniques into Druid spiritual techniques has left me thinking over the weekend.

So far I have found no trouble in applying Hermetic principles to my Druidical cosmological views. The same goes for most classical occultism I've studied (but not practiced), from Regardie to Fortune to Butler to Bardon.

What are, in your view, the most noticeable differences between Hermeticism and Revival Druidry? (I understand a book could be written on the topic, but is there anything that stands out in your mind?)

Respectfully yours,
Brigyn

replies.title: Re: Druidry and Hermeticism
replies.content: Druidry tends to value material existence and the biological side of life very highly, unlike many Hermetic schools -- the Golden Dawn adage "quit the material and seek the spiritual" would find few takers among Druids! (We'd rather awaken the spiritual within the material.) That's the one difference I can think of that matters; since modern Druidry has close ties to the Pythagorean tradition and a longstanding involvement in alchemy, the principles are mostly the same and the variations are no more extreme than those between different branches of Hermeticism, or for that matter Druidry.

replies.title: Re: Druidry and Hermeticism
replies.content:
That works for me.
Thank you kindly!

-Brigyn

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Concerning the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram for Heathens ritual you posted - you wrote in an earlier post on doing this ritual as a polytheist that you can perform it by using the name of your patron and four deities with four letter names. Does that apply here too, could I, for example, use the name FREY instead of RIGR? And if so, should I substitute the objects named later in the ritual ("Before me the Spear Gungnir...") as well or are they chosen for a specific reason? Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You could make such changes if you want to. On the other hand, if you do it the way that other Heathens are doing it, you benefit from the egregor that their practices are building, and your working will be much stronger as a result.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: How do you protect another person from a known energy vampire? Is there a natural magic approach that can disrupt it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Okay, to begin with, it's a basic principle of magical ethics not to do magic for another person without their consent. Does the other person know and approve of what you're doing? If so, there's quite a bit you can do; if not, prayer is an appropriate tool.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There are some entities I'd really rather people not pray to in my name. I got a very uncomfortable experience when some Christians I know tried to pray for my conversion, for example. Shouldn't that factor into the ethical calculation?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Praying for someone's conversion, against their will, is one thing; simply praying for someone to be protected from supernatural harm, to my mind, is another.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The other person does not believe in the existence of either magic or supernatural attack, and so is not taking measures to protect herself.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: She has the right to that belief, you know.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Hello, JMG! Am I right to assume that in the course of reincarnations as a human, the Individuality ripens from a state of ordinaryness and primitiveness by human standards toward wisdom, character and ripeness; in a similar way to the ripening of an individual human being from the ebullinence and naivete of childhood to the wisdom, knowledge and restraint of old age? W. B. Yeats wrote something roughly to this effect. Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It depends very much on the individual. Since the process of reincarnation leads each soul to become most perfectly itself -- the meaning of the title of the highest grade of initiation, Ipsissimus -- the soul as it reaches ripeness will be whatever best expresses its own nature, whether that's wisdom and restraint (characteristic of a Saturn soul), greatness and generosity (a Jupiter soul), or what have you. I'd describe it in general by saying that the soul in its human lives starts out as a generic human, indistinguishable from the mass of other humans, and life by life becomes a unique individual, the kind of person you could never mistake for anyone else.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Would this then mean that there should be more variation in earlier incarnations, and that towards the end, a lot more similarity between lives, as they express whatever that soul's unique expression is?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not necessarily, because an incarnation isn't just about what you express -- it's also about what you experience, and you need to experience the whole range of human experience before you go on.

comments.title: Whispers
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

Thanks again for providing us with your thoughts and wisdom.

The other day I was reading about a business, a very large business in fact, that to my opinion has quite exploitative employment practices. It was quite shocking to read the firsthand accounts of the people who worked there, and I knew that I would not cope with such an environment, and would probably employ a Sun Tzu technique. Anyway, the person who runs the place is extraordinarily wealthy.

I was quite outraged at the goings on, and then something whispered into my minds ear that the wealthy person in question is labouring under a curse.

And then the whole situation looked different somehow.

I won't mention names or anything like that, but it was such a strange way of looking at the situation. And it made a weird sort of sense. You mentioned years ago that you spotted a bumper sticker which claimed something along the lines of: "if you had enough, how would you know?"

It seems like an important question for sure and one worth asking - and also one that I have reflected upon long and thoughtfully. But I was curious about whether you reckon some people labour under a curse? And whether they're even aware of it? Sorry, that's two questions.... Oops! :-)

Cheers

Chris

replies.title: Re: Whispers
replies.content:
There's an old and useful saying among occultists: "character is destiny." Who you are determines, to a huge extent, what you experience. Elon Musk -- just to pull out a name at random ;-) -- is a classic study in the kind of character that Greek dramatists used to put at the center of their tragedies. He's got genuine arete, excellence, but his excellence is also his hamartia, his fatal flaw; the result is hubris and then, in due time, nemesis.

Is he under a curse? I'd say, rather, that he is his own curse; his character is such that he was pretty much guaranteed to rise far and fall hard. I'm quite sure he's not aware of it, but awareness, to borrow another occult maxim, is the universal solvent; if you get to know yourself, study your flaws as well as your strengths, and get to work changing your character, you can either avoid the worst outcomes or, if they happen, handle them with some degree of grace and land on your feet.

replies.title: Re: Whispers
replies.content: Thank you for the thoughtful reply! :-)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

I recall you once declining to interpret a frog :) so I won't ask you to interpret a parting sea of frogs recently experienced (which I totally asked for -- and was answered pointedly). My sense is that I am being directed to balance myself in terms of thought/logic versus feelings/emotion (frogs being associated with Venus, the planet of Netzach, the sphere of feelings/emotions/etc). My first thought is that entails a focus on Netzach and the 29th path (Malkuth-Netzach). For one focusing on balance in the realm of personality (the lower four spheres), are there other sequences of path-workings that are appropriate? Or other specific practices that might be helpful?

Thanks.

--David, by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Have you done a complete set of pathworkings as far as Tiphareth yet? That's the first thing to do, so the paths are open in your astral body and balancing can take place through them. Once that's done, I'd encourage you to work on the 28th, 28th, and 27th Paths -- all three paths leading up to Netzach. In your place, I'd do those one at a time: start by meditating on all the symbolism of the 29th Path, relating it to feelings and emotions, and then do a series of pathworkings on that path; then do the same with the 28th; then do the same with the 27th. Then, crucially, connect it up to Tiphareth, so that the emotional life doesn't become an end in itself.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you, John. I'm still getting my bearings on path-working, so this is very helpful.

David BTL

comments.title: Geomancy books
comments.content:
John
Picked up a couple of books on Geomancy on ABE. 'Astrological Geomancey' by Priscilla Schwei and Ralph Pestka, put out about 1990 by Llewellyn. And, 'Geomancy: A Method for Divination' by Franz Hartmann. Haven't received them yet. Waste of money or more background?
I think I found them indirectly through your 'Lost Symbol...' that I also picked up.

Coop Janitor

replies.title: Re: Geomancy books
replies.content: The Schwei and Pestka book presents a very complex and rather fussy system of astrological geomancy; some people have really good results with it, others don't. Hartmann's book is a good basic introduction, though it leaves out some of the core interpretive methods. They're both worth reading.

comments.title: Ogham cards
comments.content:
I am eagerly awaiting a set of the Murrays' Celtic Tree Oracle cards which I ordered second-hand recently. My question is, Does these cards need to be treated like Tarot cards in terms of handling and storage(like keeping them in a purpose-used cloth, not leaving anybody else touching them, etc.)or not really? Also, since it's a second-hand set, Is there anything I should do to cleanse them from any previous influence?

As usual, many, many thanks!

Manuel

replies.title: Re: Ogham cards
replies.content: Not everyone who uses Tarot cards handles them in that way, you know! As for the Ogham cards, different people do things in different ways; I treat mine the way I treat my Tarot decks, but not everyone else does. As for the cards themselves, see how they feel to you when you get them. If they have a grubby feeling, burn some frankincense, and hold each card in the smoke for a few seconds; that ought to take care of it. Otherwise, just start using them.

replies.title: Re: Ogham cards
replies.content:
Many thanks! Please ignore this question if impertinent but, May I ask how do you treat your Tarot decks?
Manuel

replies.title: Re: Ogham cards
replies.content: Depends on the deck. If it's one I use for divination and magical purposes, it goes in a cloth bag. If it's just one I study, it stays in the box it came in.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Questions on the sphere visualization for the Polytheistic Middle Pillar exercise:

1) Is it more important to get the right size or have it be inside/corresponding to your body? (...as the actress said to the bishop?) The description of the rite says eight inches diameter, but my head's maybe six inches across, and my throat maybe two, so should I shrink the spheres accordingly or have them expand beyond my actual body?

2) I'm tending to visualize the heart sphere as somewhat leftward, and the genital center feels like it's "hit" more when I pass energy down the front of my body than down the back. Is that normal, or should I try and center everything a little more?

Thanks very much!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Different people do this differently. I don't worry about keeping it inside my body, as the spheres are centers in the energy body, which extends 2-3 feet out from your skin. You might try visualizing the centers at different sizes and see what feels best to you.

2) They should be centered as much as possible. The heart center isn't the physical heart, and the genital center actually centers on the perineum rather than the genitalia themselves. Try to make the Middle Pillar run right down the midline of your body, front to back as well as side to side.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Did that tonight, and things felt much smoother. Thank you!

comments.title: Atkinson's Dynamic Thought
comments.content:
You asked me to bring my book recommendation over here for further discussion from the other blog (late in the comment cycle) so here we go:
LibriVox does volunteer-read audiobooks, and they finally finished up William Walker Atkinson's Dynamic Thought. I am still ruminating (appropriate as I have goats) over the implications, but after listening to the last chapter a second time, I hunted up your two-part series on nature spirits and realizes you basically said the same thing but from a different direction. Part One and Part Two

The LibriVox audiobook is free and so are copies of the book for those preferring to read.

I'll post things to my own DW journal once things start to crystallize into coherent thoughts, but I got the impression you are wanting to discuss this where it will be better seen.
(I hope my html works in this comment form.)

replies.title: Re: Atkinson's Dynamic Thought
replies.content: Partly it was that, and partly I figured that there isn't a 100% overlap between readers here and readers on my blog! Thank you for this; Atkinson is well worth close study, and it doesn't surprise me that there are a lot of echoes of his thinking in mine -- the Kybalion was one of the first works of occult philosophy I ever really studied.

replies.title: Re: Atkinson's Dynamic Thought
replies.content:
Out of curiosity, have you read Dynamic Thought? If Librivox hadn't done it up in audio, it would have been quite a while before I got to it.

A bit of a mystery: in the last two chapters, he mentions a sequel, but it is not on Robert's lists of known published books by Atkinson.

replies.title: Re: Atkinson's Dynamic Thought
replies.content: No, that's not one I've read! There's a lot of his stuff I haven't made time for -- for example, I haven't touched his Theron Q. Dumont books.

replies.title: Re: Atkinson's Dynamic Thought
replies.content:
I think you'd get a kick out of this one. Not just the occult philosophy, but his (now more than a century old) modern science take on the argument.

I was hoping you had, as those last two chapters are still swirling around my brain in a bit of a tangle.

replies.title: Re: Atkinson's Dynamic Thought
replies.content:
I haven't looked through Dynamic Thought, so I hadn't known that he projected a sequel. It may never have been published. If it was, it is very likely Thought Vibration, published about four months later. Atkinson, like our present host, was not one to "waste prose."

The first of these two titles is something of an oddity in Atkinson's bibliography: it was published in Los Angeles, not Chicago, and by a publisher that never published any other Atkinson title, namely, Segnogram Publishing Co. This company also published a New-Thought magazine, The Segnogram, edited by A. Victor Segno. For a small number of months it was also co-edited by Atkinson.

For some reason Atkinson appears to have found it convenient to relocate to Los Angeles for a number of months in 1907 and 1908. Then he returned to Chicago and resumed his former pattern of frequent publications, though no longer with The Psychic Research Company. His new publishers were New Thought Publishing Co. (1906), The Fiduciary Press (1907), The Lyal Book Concern (1907-8), Advanced Thought Publishing Co. (1908), and finally The Progress Co. (1908 onward). I cannot lay my hands at the moment on my note about the matter, but (IIRC) his partner in The Psychic Research Company got in legal trouble right at the time that Atkinson left Chicago for Los Angeles. Presumably the legal troubles were resolved after a few months, and Atkinson felt it safe to return to Chicago and resume his former life of writing and publishing.

If you take a look at Thought Vibration, dfr1973, let me know if it looks like the sequel to you.

comments.title: Gods
comments.content:
In your experience, do we choose our Gods or do they choose us?
I ask because as an unorthodox Christian, I'm sure at least some of the Olympians have reached out and blessed me recently. With no objections from the Trinity I'm pleased to say.
Many, many thanks for the fine curriculum in Learning Ritual Magic also.
A token of my gratitude can be found in the tip jar shortly.

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content: Thank you! Very often the gods do the choosing, and when they do so, it's good manners (if nothing more!) to respond appropriately.

comments.title: Planetary Intelligence vs. Spirit
comments.content:
In your experience, what is the difference between the Planetary Intelligence and the Planetary Spirit named in Agrippa's Three Books of Occult Philosophy?

Henry Cornelius Agrippa seems to suggest that the Imtelligence be used for "good" or benefic purposes, and associates them with a planet that is dignified, while the Spirit be used for "evil" purposes associated with their respective planets ill-dignified. However, in the Golden Dawn systems of magic (and their offshoots) I have heard that the Spirits are used for material purposes while the Intelligences are used for visionary or astral type results.

Can the Spirits be used for "good" material results or would you use the Intelligence instead?

Thanks,

Ben

replies.title: Re: Planetary Intelligence vs. Spirit
replies.content:
In my experience the Golden Dawn approach seems to be correct -- of course that's the system of magic I've used the most, so I'm biased! The spirits aren't evil; rather, they're potent but very nearly mindless. To work on the material plane for good ends -- and I don't recommend ever doing anything else -- you first contact the Intelligence of the planet, interact with it, and ask it to direct the Spirit of the planet to bring about the material goal. That way you get the results you want. If you want to work on the astral or mental planes, you just evoke the Intelligence and leave it at that.

A couple of examples might help clarify things. If you want to consecrate a talisman of Venus, let's say, you'd make the talisman during the day and hour of Venus when Venus is well dignified; then, in a different day and hour of Venus, Venus not being afflicted at the time, you'd summon the Intelligence of Venus in the appropriate Divine Names, speak to her, and ask her to command the Spirit of Venus to place Venereal energies in the talisman. (This is a *very* potent method of talisman consecration, btw; some of my most effective talismans were consecrated this way.)

If you want to work on making yourself more lovable, on the other hand -- which is of course the most effective form of love spell -- you'd simply choose a day and hour of Venus when Venus is unafflicted, do a working to summon the Intelligence of Venus, and ask her help in your work. Since your personality is an astral phenomenon, and the attitudes that usually keep people from being lovable are on the mental plane, that's well within an Intelligence's ambit.

comments.title: Kundalini yoga
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
We have a couple of really lovely yogis on our island and they teach Kundalini yoga. These people are so bright and sweet that they seem to shine and they seem extremely innocent. I study yoga with them and have had some interesting experiences, flashes of visions and such.

Is their system of yoga a suitable practice for someone studying Restoration Druidry? What is your opinion of Kundalini yoga? I have heard that the yogi who started the practice was quite corrupt but cannot see anything of the sort in these two people.

Thanks,
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: Kundalini yoga
replies.content: Kundalini yoga is a very powerful system, and very dangerous if it's done wrong. It should never be mixed with any other form of magical or subtle-energy practice -- if you take up kundalini yoga, in other words, it would be wise to stop doing magical rituals and anything else that moves subtle energy, and restrict your Druidry to prayer and seasonal rituals.

replies.title: Re: Kundalini yoga
replies.content:
Dear JMG,
Wow, good thing you are there to be asked as I had no idea I had strayed into danger. There is a beginner session of Tai Chi starting at the community school and I wanted to try it. Is Tai Chi okay with my Druid magical practice? I do not know much about Tai Chi and do not know if they move subtle energies.

My Druid magical practice is going well and I am slowly working my way through the Druid Magic Handbook. I am enjoying the work and getting good results. I am becoming happier and more contented. Thank you for helping us beginners!
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: Kundalini yoga
replies.content:
Whether t'ai chi involves moving energies depends on whether you get more than the very basic forms of the exercise. If it does, you can land yourself in trouble that way too -- I certainly did. (I practiced t'ai chi for ten years, and had to drop it when the conflict between that set of energy work and my Druid magic started causing serious health problems.)

AODA has a system of qigong called Nine Hazels Qigong which was invented by Archdruid of Fire Adam Robersmith, and it's entirely compatible with the magical work you're doing. You might see if someone on the AODA list can point you to instructions, if that interests you.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I managed to miss last week, when the huge news item, at least in my social circle, was the burning of the Museum in Brazil due to a lack of physical maintainance. I wonder, though, if there might not be more to that, as the archologists are going on about priceless treasures lost, and those would certainly include religious and magical items.

First, it seems to me that if gods were annoyed at the paraphanalia of their worship being in collections or on display, that influencing politicians not to allocate repair funds would be a fairly simple matter if the politicians had no protections from that influence (say, from another god). Is this something gods can do?
Second, it seems to me that most religions-probably all-require sacrifice. Is a sacrifice of this sort of collection something some gods would appreciate, and ask their worshipers for?

If either of these are true, it would behoove those of us who value some local museum to consider taking precautionary measures, wouldn't it? What sort of measures would be useful?

BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It would depend entirely on the local museum, and the local gods. If the gods whose holy things are on display in your museum are pissed enough, yes, they could certainly do that, possibly to receive an offering, possibly to take back the sacred powers embodied in the objects. If you're concerned about your local museum, find out whose artifacts are there, and talk to the people who follow the same traditions to see if there's anything that should be done.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not *quite* what you ask (or about magic) but pertinent: Brazil's corrupt, and lazy and sloppy even when not corrupt (I've worked in very ordinary places where fire prevention didn't work as it should either), especially about high culture, so I think it may have had no noteworthy causes. If there's more to it than meets the common eye, it may be related to the risk of military dictatorship/civil war having had a recent slight increase with a Far-Rightist presidential candidate having been cut up (non-fatally). (If  there was actual fighting, nearly everyone worth noting would be Christian, and I don't *think* the current sides even correspond to the Catholic/Evangelical split.)

comments.title: Deities
comments.content:
Hi John,
Do you have particularly deities you prefer to work with, and if so why? Or conversely do you choose deities depending on what your magical intensions are?
Many thanks
AJ

replies.title: Re: Deities
replies.content: I find it easiest to work with a single pantheon, and as a Druid working in the Druid Revival tradition, it makes sense to me to work with the rather odd mix of Welsh and ancient Celtic deities that Druid Revival traditions generally work with. (You can find these discussed in some detail in my book The Druid Magic Handbook.) Any reasonably complete pantheon has gods or goddesses who correspond to anything you might need to pray for or work magic for. So, like old-fashioned Druids for the last couple of hundred years, I invoke Hu the Mighty, Ced the earth mother, and Hesus chief of tree-spirits as a matter of course, and then call on other deities from the same pantheon for workings specific to their natures.

comments.title: Beginning Breath Control
comments.content: What practices or information would you recommend for someone learning how to build energy within themselves?  Pranayama?

replies.title: Re: Beginning Breath Control
replies.content: That depends completely on what you want to do with the energy and what system of energy work you have in mind. Pranayama is great if you want to practice yoga; it's a bad idea if you want to do some other kinds of work. Tell me the job you want done and I can probably tell you what tools you need to do it!

replies.title: Re: Beginning Breath Control
replies.content: Not the OP, and apologies if this is outside of courtesy's remit, but what if I desire freedom from limiting thoughts and attitudes?

replies.title: Re: Beginning Breath Control
replies.content:
You don't address that by building energy on the etheric plane. You address it with work on the mental plane, which is where limiting thoughts and attitudes live, and also on the astral plane, which is where the emotions linked to limiting thoughts and attitudes reside.

If I may offer a recommendation, a New Thought correspondence course I've just put online, free of charge, might be of help to you, as shaking off limiting thoughts and attitudes is one of the things New Thought methods do best.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm working with the Hellenic SoP (as outlined in the last magic monday), and have some observations: first, the effect is far stronger than any other version I've done yet. I'm working with the elemental cross, and introducing the elements one at a time, but the elemental cross feels quite powerful on its own.

I've also noticed that the domains of my life ruled Artemis, Aphrodite, and Hermes are improving quite a lot, much more so than merely from the working. I'm not sure about the domain of Dionysus because I'm not a hundred percent sure of what his domain is, which raises my first question: By invoking these particular gods, do I change the ritual such that the effect on their domain is more pronounced?

After a couple days (maybe to make sure I'm not a flake), I found myself being told to stop reading a book on Greek religion and start practicing. I was surprised, dropped the book at this very audible command from a god, and then when I picked it up again it was open to Artemis, so I guessed that was who was talking. This was a little odd, but she then insisted I had enough to be able to do what she wanted. I opened the book to look, for prayer, and found myself staring at a page on libations, so I assumed that's what she wanted. I've now been making daily offerings to her, and I seem to be getting responses. Do you think by practicing the Hellenic SoP I've drawn her attention?

I also find myself in the category of people who are baffled on how to relate to the gods. Do you have any advice on how to proceed with this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
When you invoke the gods, you draw their attention, and you establish a link through which they can more easily act on you. That is to say, you're getting what you asked for -- be glad of that!

As for how to proceed with the gods, it sounds to me as though you have a very good source of information on that -- the gods themselves. These days, especially, they're used to dealing with humans who have no idea how to handle a normal, healthy relationship with a god. Pray to them, ask them for guidance, and then pay attention to whatever answers you get, and you should be fine.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What, if any, is the difference between invocation and prayer?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Invocation is prayer used with concentrated intention in a magical ritual.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Thanks for another Magic Monday!

From questions and answers here it seems that it's relatively easy to affect another person by basically thinking about them intensively or with a good deal of passion, from giving them nightmares to actually causing physical pain all the way across an ocean (in case of a reader with a cousin, who likes him a bit too much). In view of that, I would expect that let's say tyrants, who are hated and cursed by millions of people, would have a pretty short and miserable lives but it's often not the case. Why?

Along the same lines, if even lay people can affect other people so much, I'd expect that operating mages can do a lot more. Stalin, Hitler, Tamerlane, etc. have greatly upset (to put it mildly) millions of people each. Statistically, there should be at least some strong mages among those, which, once again, should have resulted in some sort of action quickly curtailing activities of a tyrant in question but history doesn't seem to support this.

The only explanation I can come up with is that there are some sort of "pact with the devil" type arrangements with some higher being(s) that protect the tyrants from the blowback but I would very much like to hear your thoughts on that.

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There's a much simpler explanation, but it requires discarding a common bad habit -- the notion that because we think of someone as evil, that opinion must have been shared by everyone at the time. Let's plunge straight into Godwin's Law territory and talk about Hitler. Millions of Germans adored him. They thought of him as the man who ended Germany's economic crisis, put millions of people back to work, and gave their country its pride again after the humiliation of Versailles. It wasn't until after the war was over that everyone scurried around insisting that no, no, they hated him all along. The millions who adored him included a lot of capable operative mages; a lot of those mages ended up in the Ahnenerbe, the branch of the SS that handled occult research; and the Nuremberg rallies, among many other things, were pretty clearly designed as magical workings to empower the Nazi party and its leaders. Thus it's no wonder that Hitler lasted as long as he did, and went under only when his own military incompetence doomed his regime.

The same principle applies to others. Most people are fine with tyrants as long as they personally benefit, or at least aren't too badly inconvenienced by his tyranny; smart tyrants, in turn, know that the success of their regimes depends on their willingness to listen to their followers' nonpolitical grievances and hand out goodies on a regular basis. Half the reason that democracies fall into the hands of tyrants is that politicians in a democratic society too often get so caught up in partisan bickering that they forget that Joe and Jane Average are far more concerned about the things that affect their daily life than about ornate political ideologies.

comments.title: Past Life Trauma
comments.content:
Do you have advice for someone who has an undercurrent of odd fear in their heart? My worries don't tend to manifest in reality, in fact when bad things happen I'm always almost completely blindsided, but still I feel like a useless animal fear is constantly circulating in my heart. It really can zap the joy out of living, but doesn't seem to have any other purpose. It may relate to some rather horrific past life experiences that I specifically remember. It is like I'm processing trauma from a prior incarnation, without direct bearing on my own.

Is there good ways to deal with such past life related fear rather than just clenching my teeth? I've tried employing Transactional Analysis to see what sort of games I'm playing, and it doesn't seem like a game, more like a deranged animal force. I do not seem to have control over it, and am not sure the best course of action and so I ask you your thoughts, about dealing with unwarranted fears and past-life issues.

replies.title: Re: Past Life Trauma
replies.content: Have you tried journaling? Writing out a conversation, or a series of conversations, with your fear is one way to help sort things out.

replies.title: Re: Past Life Trauma
replies.content:
If I might supplement JMG’s suggestion- that sounds a great deal like Generalized Anxiety Disorder, and if it’s a persistent problem and your circumstances allow, you might consider addressing it via “mundane” means- i.e., seeing a therapist.

Physical exercise and mindfulness meditation, also, can help against “free floating” anxiety (and are usually healthy habits in general, of course.)

comments.title: Paths of Wisdom question
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I'm reading through Paths of Wisdom, and I ran across the section where you discuss the Sphere of Sensation. You describe how mages can map different magical diagrams onto the Sphere, and mention that some mages will map the Zodiac onto their Sphere.

What's the benefit one gets from doing that? I figure it's far beyond my skill level currently, but I find the idea fascinating.

Also, when I meditate on a prospective working, I tend to rehearse the ritual in my mind. You've mentioned doing magic in your dreams to good effect, and doing the Sphere of Protection entirely in the astral plane. So then, would my mental rehearsal have any kind of effect, in and of itself?

Thanks,
Cliff

replies.title: Re: Paths of Wisdom question
replies.content:
1) It's a very complex mode of magical working. With practice, you can direct your attention to the portion of your aura that reflects a given part of the heavens, charge that with energy, and have that work like an instant talisman.

2) Yes, and it's a good thing. You're basically reinforcing the pattern of the ritual, so that when you actually do it, you're moving along channels you've already established, resulting in stronger rituals.

comments.title: Plants inside the house
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've been looking for an answer to this question in previous posts to no avail, so although it sounds a bit silly I thought I'd better ask you.

Are there any plants that one should avoid keeping inside the house? I have no plants or pets, except for a couple of orchids.

Main reason to ask is my conflicted feelings about bonsai. On the pictures in books they look beautiful, but I feel quite sad when I see them... I feel it's almost like torture... I started to read a bit about the things they do to the trees to achieve a certain look and quite frankly, it was upsetting - using acids, scarring the trunk with a saw, stuff like that...am I reading too much into this? What do you think?

I wonder if you could suggest some good books to learn a bit more about plants/herbs. Would you recommend your Encyclopaedia of Natural Magic?

Many thanks for your time!

replies.title: Re: Plants inside the house
replies.content:
If you don't have a good feeling about bonsai, trust that. Plants are living beings and should be treated as such.

The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic is a good place to start learning about the magical dimensions of plants; of course there are many other good books on the subject as well.

replies.title: Re: Plants inside the house
replies.content:
Unless you live in the South or Southwest, bonsai won’t survive as houseplants anyway without HUGE amounts of artificial light. And if you live in the South or Southwest you’ll have to provide an unheated or frigidly air-conditioned, dimly lit room for their natural periods of dormancy. They really aren’t houseplants.

You might try palms, if you have good light, dracaena marginata which looks sort of like a palm tree if you have a bit of imagination, dracaena fragrans (a corn plant—which is what it looks like!—in your living room!), or cycads, which are just adorable with little toy dinosaurs “strutting” in their pots.

comments.title: LBRP advice
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

After 4 months of daily divination with improving results I started practicing the LBRP as instructed in your book The Celtic Golden Dawn. I had a good sensation during all the ritual, feeling that I was redirecting the energy that traversed my body with my fingers while drawing the pentagrams and noticing the different "qualities" of each element. A really intense sensation. For no particular reason I was visualizing big pentagrams on the walls delimiting my apartment not just the walls of the room I was in. Also I was doing only the banishing version of the ritual as feeling it like more "safe" for me being that really novice I am now.
So far, so good.
But on commenting my experience with my wife she explained me that she had lost his connection with the gods or saints she use to pray and that she never before had felt that sensation of disconnection. Immediately I stopped the practice of the ritual. After some days he regained his connection.
It's possible to restrict the effect of the ritual only to one room using the adequate visualization? Or may it be safer for me to change to the Sphere of Protection ritual instead?


Many thanks for hosting these magic mondays. I'm learning a LOT.
Sincerely,
Sergi

replies.title: Re: LBRP advice
replies.content:
Of course you can limit the range of the effect -- just don't project the pentagrams beyond the room in which you're working. Better still, trace them well inside the walls of the room, as though hanging in the air.

You should also do the invoking as well as the banishing; just doing one will unbalance you, and the space in which you work.

replies.title: Re: LBRP advice
replies.content:
"You should also do the invoking as well as the banishing; just doing one will unbalance you, and the space in which you work."

does this apply to the polytheist LBRP that you shared on this blog mid January? I've only been banishing for many months now -- do you think it would be wise to start invoking as well, or is that unnecessary with the specific ritual form you shared?

replies.title: Re: LBRP advice
replies.content: Yes, you should be invoking as well. You can either do the LIRP in the polytheist mode, or you can do the LBRP followed by the polytheist Middle Pillar, which is a different kind of invocation and balances things out very nicely.

replies.title: Re: LBRP advice
replies.content:
Is it common practice to banish one day then invoke the next, switching back and forth?

Also, how does invoking, vs banishing, effect the middle pillar exercise?

replies.title: Re: LBRP advice
replies.content: If you're doing the Middle Pillar, always do the banishing LRP first, since the MP is itself an invocation. Alternating invoking and banishing on alternate days is a standard practice when you haven't gotten to the MP yet.

replies.title: Re: LBRP advice
replies.content:
Hold the phone! I just started doing the Heathen LBRP and Central Axis rituals literally this morning, so it's quite timely that you mention this balancing of banishing and summoning now.

Should one do the banishing, then the Central Axis, then the summoning? Or banish one day and summon the next? What's the procedure to achieve balance?

replies.title: Re: LBRP advice
replies.content: If you're doing the Central Axis, that's your invocation; banish, then do the Central Axis, and you'll be fine. You need to alternate invoking and banishing when you're not doing another invocation.

comments.title: Personal Magnetism
comments.content:
Since your most recent post got into New Thought, I was wondering what you thought about the ethics of one of the more popular New Thought topics: Personal Magnetism.

Many of the important New Thought Authors have books on personal magnetism that are clear about their intentions of influencing people's actions by force of will.

On one hand, that sounds a little bit like the "trying to override someone else's will" that you've warned against.

One the other hand, since consciousnesses are not isolated from one another under normal circumstances, it seems like strengthening one's own will would necessarily lead to greater influence on others, and seeking this result intentionally doesn't sound so different from someone joining Toastmasters to improve their public speaking ability.

What do you think about this?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Personal Magnetism
replies.content: There's a difference between strengthening your own will and letting that have its natural effect on your surroundings, and studying how to make other people do what you want. If you strengthen your will, you subtly encourage everyone else to strengthen theirs; if you weaken other people's will -- which is what you have to do in order to force them to obey you -- you subtly weaken your own.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Hi JMG, I'm leaving my name out of this because of the content.  I'm studying Druid magic along the lines of the DMH and the AODA.  I'd like to do some magic with the goal of becoming more financially secure.  I'm not going for anything fancy, but I'd like to clear enough dough so I'm not crossing my fingers and toes hoping my checks don't bounce every month.  A huge part of the issue is my husband, despite his competence in many fields, is older and partially disabled and has not been able to find a job that pays over $13 an hour.  Is there any specific magic you'd suggest for my situation?  What magical time of day should I be looking at?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Money magic is one of the two kinds of magic that most often blow up in people's faces -- the other is love magic. A vast amount depends on what you're trying to do, and why. Most of the people who ask me about money magic are trying to find some way to live beyond their means without having to work more, and that's pretty much guaranteed to fail -- especially in a contracting economy and a civilization in decline.

Still, I can offer you two pieces of advice, one practical, one magical. The practical point is that almost everyone in the US is going to be a lot poorer in the future, so the sooner you get used to living with a lot less money, the better. The magical point is that if you're going to do magic for money, focus your intention on trying to find ways to earn more money, not on trying to get money without having to earn it.

If you're up for earning more money, this Friday before sunrise, take a piece of brown paper (part of a grocery bag is fine) and tear it, don't cut it, into a square three inches or so on a side. Write your full name seven times on the paper, one below the other:

Ermengarde Eudora Smith
Ermengarde Eudora Smith
Ermengarde Eudora Smith
Ermengarde Eudora Smith
Ermengarde Eudora Smith
Ermengarde Eudora Smith
Ermengarde Eudora Smith

Then turn the paper a quarter turn, and write across the top of your name the following phrase seven times:

I earn more money
I earn more money
I earn more money
I earn more money
I earn more money
I earn more money
I earn more money

Fold the paper in half, folding it toward you, then turn it a quarter turn and fold it toward you again, so it's folded in quarters.

Then, at dawn exactly, place that under a green candle, light the candle, and say, "I am willing to do whatever I have to do, without harm to myself or anyone else, to earn the money I need." Let the candle burn for seven minutes and then snuff it -- don't blow it out. Burn it again at dawn each Friday morning until it's gone, leaving the paper beneath it. During the first week, an opportunity to earn more money will come your way, and even though it probably won't be much money and it will involve doing something you don't like doing, do it anyway -- that will lock the energies into place. More opportunities will come your way thereafter.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I don’t know if this is a magical or philosophical question: what’s a good way to keep from being chronically annoyed by chronic stupidity?

I read somewhere that in the U.S., at least, for the last 30 years IQ has been dropping by 7 points per generation. I would not doubt it. President Mountain Dew is in our future!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's a philosophical question, and there are various ways to do it depending on your personality. The first thing is to make sure you don't secretly enjoy watching other people being stupid, because it makes you feel smart -- this is extremely common, and it guarantees that you'll keep on feeling the way you do because you actually enjoy it. If that's not what's going on, try paying attention to how much time and effort you put into getting upset about things that you can't actually do anything about, until you notice that that, too, is a kind of chronic stupidity... ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks, John!

I did not begin to notice rampant stupidity ‘till the early 80’s , also the time Americans began to grow fatter and crazier. I suspect high fructose corn syrup affects the mind as well as the body. And since I can’t afford to leave, I’m probably stupid, fat, and crazy just like everyone else here!

comments.title: Confused
comments.content:
Hello JMG

I’m a bit confused, and was wondering if you could clarify something for me: You have on more than one occasion stated that “love spells” are a very bad idea, and can backfire spectacularly. OK,that makes sense. It is sort of like drugging someone’s drink, I suspect. Ick. But also on more than one occasion, you have suggested “honeypot” spells, and provided a link to another person’s website, where one may find spells to “sweeten the relationship” between two people. What is the difference?

Signed,
somewhat confused person who would like to be closer to another person but does not want to try to force him to do anything against his will

replies.title: Re: Confused
replies.content:
A sweetening spell is used when you're already in some kind of relationship with someone -- for example, you're neighbors (that's a relationship!) who can't stand each other -- and you want to do something to improve the relationship. It won't just sweeten the other person, it'll also sweeten you, since it usually takes two or more people to make a bad relationship. In the same way, if you were married and the marriage was going sour, a sweetening spell might well be appropriate.

If you want to be closer to someone, your best bet is to work love magic on yourself; remember that the one kind of love magic that always works and never backfires is the kind that focuses on making you more lovable. That's uncomfortable, as it involves confronting your less lovable aspects -- which we all have -- and your fears that you're not lovable -- which we also all have -- but that's what works. You might consider, with this in mind, writing your own name seven times on a square piece of paper you've torn from a paper bag or the like -- it must be torn on all four sides, not cut -- and then putting it into a jar, filling the jar with honey, and putting it in your bedroom close to where you sleep. The goal of the working is to make you a sweeter person, so that other people -- very likely including the person you want to get close to -- will want to get closer to you.

replies.title: Re: Confused
replies.content: Thanks! I will try working on myself!

comments.title: Unbaptism
comments.content:
Apologies, Mr. Greer, for asking so late, and if this has been answered before, but it came up with friends today.

Is there any way to reverse a baptism? A catholic confirmation? Any rituals for leaving the Church?

i'm not to sure about this, not having needed it myself, but my friend seems to have trouble, and is seeking help in this regard.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Unbaptism
replies.content:
Does your friend worship some other deity, or is he or she willing to do so? That's the classic way of doing it. Your friend can get a bunch of friends together who can handle Pagan worship and are minded to celebrate, and prepare a big tasty meal. When everyone's gathered, your friend stands up in the middle of everyone and says something like this:

"I formally renounce my baptism; I am no longer a Christian or a member of the Church. I formally renounce my confirmation and will never again use my confirmation name. I formally renounce the Holy Spirit and all its works. Begone! Begone! Begone! I pray to another." Then your friend takes a cup of wine, ale, or mead (depending on the deity of choice) and pours a libation onto the ground, saying, "(Name of deity), receive this offering! I ask you to be my patron god/goddess, to bless and guide and protect me from this day forever." Your friend can go on to pray at greater length if that seems appropriate.

Everyone applauds, and then everyone sits down to the feast, which opens with a grace said to your friend's patron deity. Before anyone eats, a small portion of the food is put on a plate and set somewhere higher than the table, as the deity's portion -- and later, after everyone else has gone home, your friend takes the deity's portion, thanks the deity, and eats the portion as an act of communion. Eating food offered to Pagan deities is among the things utterly forbidden to Christians, and this marks the completion of the rite.

replies.title: Re: Unbaptism
replies.content:
I actually knew someone who returned to the conservative church in which he had been raised, waited for the altar call and went up front to publicly announce that he was no longer a Christian. Then walked out.

For non-Christian readers --- the altar call is the portion of the service in which congregants are invited to come to the front of the church to accept Jesus into their lives. It is pretty much an evangelical or Pentecostal activity. The minister and church members usually respond with exclamations and prayers of thanksgiving for the newly 'saved' person.

Rita

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have done my first few scryings (as in the Druid Magic Handbook) and find imagery not readily forthcoming. Only one rather vague image arose last time.

Is there value in attempting scrying outside of the grove ritual, simply to practice/build up aptitude?

Also, what do you think of approaching scrying with a specific question in mind?

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Scrying takes practice. I don't recommend scrying outside of the grove ritual, but you can certainly practice visualization -- for example, spend ten minutes a day imagining yourself taking a walk through a landscape you know well, visualizing it as clearly as possible. Keep that up for a while and your scryings will become much more clear.

Scrying with a specific question is certainly an option, but it's important also to make room for purely spontaneous scryings.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: The universe appears big enough to accommodate for all sorts of afterlife scenarios; besides the druid afterlife what other types of life after death “ideas” do you like or have been inspired to contemplate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oh, I suspect that most religions tell the truth about what happens to their faithful after death. I find reincarnation the most interesting option, partly because I remember a number of my previous lives, and partly because I like being incarnate; but I don't doubt that various gods gather their believers into various discarnate existences such as heaven.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When tracing the signs for the elements in the SOP, should one deliberately direct energy to fingertips to form the tracery, or is the physical motion enough?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you're visualizing the colored light, that and the movement should be quite adequate.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good Evening all.  Most of the time I don’t feel any need to ask a question as it has probably already been covered and asked more artfully that I generally managed.  That being said I have been amazed at how often things on Magic Monday seem to tie into whatever I am working on.  The day after I highlighted “Don’t monkey with the Buzzsaw” in the copy of WE Butler’s The Magician, John uses it in a comment response. 

That being said I had a few things crop up this week. 
 
The tie between physical culture and occultism has been mentioned over the last several weeks.  Is that something that could be added as the “something else” to the Heathen Golden Dawn book?

You mention in Learning Ritual Magic that books on building will power were common in the 19th century.  Are there any you recommend or used heavily?  Along those lines is there on aimed at teaching children?  Something that could be adapted into a homeschool curriculum.

Oh and if there are children in the house when is it safe to introd…
Yes my inner 6 year old likes coming out why do you ask?
Other Dave

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Your inner six year old probably needs a spanking. ;-)

The idea of putting some of the magical dimensions of physical culture into the Heathen Golden Dawn project is interesting, and I'll discuss it with my coauthor when he gets back from army maneuvers. As for books on building willpower, I read a bunch of them back in the day, got the basic principles, and worked up my own exercises. The basic rule with will exercises is that you do something for no reason at all, just because you decide to do it. For example, decide that you're going to be at a certain place at an inconvenient time, and be there on the dot!

I'll review the will exercises I know and see if I have any ideas for children. The whole subject of will, self-discipline, and training might be worth a blog post, in fact.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In my experience, children don't lack willpower--they just don't direct it in the direction the adults would prefer. If any of us as adults had the sheer determination of a toddler learning to walk--well I wouldn't get in that person's way. The same child who won't memorize the multiplication tables will cheerfully learn every fact about their favorite dinosaur or the game stats of their favored sports team.

Unfortunately completely child led education does not work as well as readers of _Summerhill_ may wish. My grandson was in a Waldorf school that was not well run. Waldorf education calls for reading instruction, but not before 3rd grade. He graduated 3rd still unable to read, despite really wanting to, because the teacher thought he would learn when ready. Transferred to public school he went from _Hop on Pop_ (preschool level) to the Lemony Snicket series ( about 7th grade reading level) in one year, with the help of a teacher who actually taught.

That said, children whose natural wills have been weakened by forced concentration on things that bore them may be happy to learn how to refocus. I certainly would have, I think. And learning the skill of delaying gratification would be good for any age--this could be a game, like who can make their lollipop last longest, things like that.

Rita

comments.title: Can a lake be a god.
comments.content: I live on the shore of Lake Superior.  It feels like a god to me.  Not as though there is a spirit of the lake, but the lake itself is the entity.   Is this possible?

replies.title: Re: Can a lake be a god.
replies.content: Do you mean can the lake be the body of a local god? Sure. It used to be quite common in ancient times for people to offer worship to bodies of water as deities.

replies.title: Re: Can a lake be a god.
replies.content: For what it's worth, I just spent the entire summer on the shore of lake superior and had several encounters with something that I would characterize exactly as you have. A very potent force, but also, in my experience anyway, a kind one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: This may be the most occult of occult questions.  How can you know who and what you are?  I know the basics:  when in the late 1920’s my grandparents were starving to death in Virginia, they headed west to Penna rather than north to Canada, and so I’m a U. S. citizen and not a Canadian one.  I know my race, my sex (a stumper for some folks these days) my age, and my allergy.  But I don’t really know ME.  Suggestions?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you spend the rest of your life wrestling with that question, reflecting on it, and poking at it, you might begin to make some progress toward answering it. That's why Gnothi Seauton -- "Know Yourself" in ancient Greek -- was written above the portal to the oracle at Delphi; it's the most difficult of all knowledge to attain, and the most useful.

comments.title: Occultism and religion
comments.content:
What differentiates occultism from religion, other than Renaissance politics?

- Dylan

replies.title: Re: Occultism and religion
replies.content: Ah, definitions! It really does depend on who you ask.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When I'm doing the sphere of protection ritual, am I invoking and banishing  my body, or is the entire space I'm doing it in also being invoked and banished?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: None of the above. You're invoking and banishing elemental energies, and asking them to do certain things for you.

comments.title: so many questions!
comments.content:
John, I procede with another installment of my 'so many questions' serie.

I remember your mentioning of, in some episode of The Kek Wars,the use of magic by some groups that had tossed aside the history, with assorted interpretations and teachings,and kept only what concerned actual practice. I was wondering, has perhaps something similar occurred with the practice of fasting? I assume that fasting is indeed magical? Have you ever tried it, or studied it? For some unknown reason it's something that I'm eager to try out, and will probably end up taking the plunge, doing it my own way, learning as I go as I always do. But I figured why not making use of this great space you so very kindly host.

best regards,

Lorenzo

replies.title: Re: so many questions!
replies.content: Fasting has several dimensions. I've done it for health reasons, with good results; I don't use it in my magical work, though some people do. I'd advise doing it a little at a time -- start out with a 24 hour fast, and see how your body deals with that -- and be sure to stay well hydrated.

replies.title: Re: fasting
replies.content: I was stunned to read in the most recent Parade newspaper insert that the TV-famous "Dr. Oz" recommends "intermittent fasting".  What is that?  It is the practice of not eating for *12 hours* at a time (say, between 7 PM and 7 AM). He points out that this is not TOO difficult, since we'll be sleeping most of the time.  THAT's a FAST?  That's just the way I was raised.  Apparently, there's some significant fraction of our population the stuffs their pie-hole every waking hour (and may wake for a mid-night snack).  It's mind-boggling.

comments.title: Planetary Intelligences vs Deities
comments.content:
Is the Planetary Intelligence the same as the deity associated with that planet? For example, is the Planetary Intelligence of Venus the goddess Venus? I have had the habit this summer of going out at night when Venus is clearly visible and talking to her. I’ve also done this with Jupiter to a lesser extent and recently have been looking at Mars in an attempt at a connection. I generally start with a communication based on how these planets are aspected in my birth chart and go from there. Am I communing with the Planetary Intelligences or with the associated deities?

Thank you,
Yanocoches in CO

replies.title: Re: Planetary Intelligences vs Deities
replies.content: The planetary intelligences aren't deities, they're beings subordinate to the deities. You're probably praying to the deities, but of course I wasn't there at the time and so can't be sure.

comments.title: Emergence
comments.content: I'm curious, in your view, are storm systems a type of nature spirit? How would one treat communicating with them differently than the sorts of nature spirits that stay put in places like forests and other wild areas?

replies.title: Re: Emergence
replies.content: No, storm systems are one of the things that nature spirits do. Specifically they're things that the elemental spirits of air do. Elementals aren't as place-bound as some other spirits, and air elementals in particular ride the winds around the globe.

comments.title: meditation, prayer, through writing
comments.content:
In the most recent Cosmic Doctrine comment section I noted that I seem to have decent focus while both contemplating a text with pencil (and margin) in hand and in following my own thoughts on a subject if I write my way through it.

According to the way you teach meditation and going by what you know of prayer and meditation in general, would this be considered a detour or less than ideal practice? I can kinda liken it to letting my hand walk while I meditate (or converse with a god? Not sure, I've not actually tried it, but I've conversed with my muse before and geniuses/daimons are in that general direction perhaps?).

Thanks :)

replies.title: Re: meditation, prayer, through writing
replies.content: Not at all. If that works for you, it works for you.
